questions.question: Define Authentication and differentiate it from Identification. Also list two common methods used for authentication.
keyPoints: Authentication verifies the identity claimed by a user
Identification is the initial step of claiming an identity
Common authentication methods: password (knowledge), biometric (inherence), token (possession)

questions.question: Define Authorization and explain its role in enforcing access rights after a user has been authenticated. Provide an example of an authorization model.
keyPoints: Authorization grants specific privileges based on authenticated identity
Operates after authentication in the access control process
Example models: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), Access Control Lists (ACL)

questions.question: Explain the CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) as core functional security requirements. For each component, describe at least one mechanism used to achieve it in an information system.
keyPoints: Confidentiality: encryption, access controls, data classification
Integrity: hashing, digital signatures, checksums, version control
Availability: redundancy, fault‑tolerant systems, backups, load balancing
Trade‑offs: security measures may impact performance or usability

questions.question: Discuss the AAA framework (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) in the context of functional security requirements. Explain how Availability is addressed, and describe the concept of Non‑repudiation and mechanisms that provide it.
keyPoints: AAA: Authentication (who you are), Authorization (what you can do), Accounting (what you did)
Availability ensures systems and data are accessible when needed; mechanisms include redundancy, SLAs, intrusion detection
Non‑repudiation prevents denial of performed actions; mechanisms: digital signatures, timestamps, audit logs
Together, AAA and Non‑repudiation provide accountability and trust
